text: The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the gastrocolic ligament using a harmonic scalpel to control the small gastric vessels. Subsequently, the dissection of the main trunk of the splenic artery is initiated, with robotic assistance aiding in the precision of this maneuver.

The surgeon clamps the main trunk of the splenic artery to reduce the spleen's volume, facilitating safer dissection. The first and second small arterial branches are reconnected to preserve the vascularization of the lower part of the spleen, which remains intact.
The surgeon applies a clip to control the main trunk of the splenic artery and then removes the clamp to verify the correct dissection of the arterial branches.

The main trunk of the splenic vein is visually detached from the tail of the pancreas and circled by a vessel loop, allowing for traction to minimize injury risk. The tail of the pancreas is gently detached from the vessels, with grasping on the main trunk of the artery and vein.

The surgeon divides the main trunk of the ascending artery and vein. The lower pole of the spleen is visible, showing signs of recovering vascularization. The vascular hilum of the upper pole of the spleen is detached from the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform parenchymal transection along the demarcation line, minimizing bleeding. The lower pole of the spleen is separated from the upper pole, which contains a large cystic lesion.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to control small bleeding from the stump. The inferior and superior ligaments of the spleen are divided, allowing the upper part of the spleen to be separated. Vessel loops are removed, and the spleen specimen is extracted through a small incision using an endobag.

The surgeon reconstructs the splenophrenic ligament to prevent torsion of the remnant spleen on its vascular pole. A hemostatic matrix is applied to control small bleeding from the remnant spleen, marking the completion of the procedure.
